inlineContent.text: By default,
will cache all properties (and listen to
changes) of the remote object, and proxy all signals that get
emitted. This behaviour can be changed by passing suitable
[flags
] when the proxy is created. If the proxy is for a
well-known name, the property cache is flushed when the name owner
vanishes and reloaded when a name owner appears.
The unique name owner of the proxy’s name is tracked and can be read from
[property
]. Connect to the
[signal[Object](//documentation/gobject/object/onnotify(flags:handler:)] signal to get notified of changes.
Additionally, only signals and property changes emitted from the current name
owner are considered and calls are always sent to the current name owner.
This avoids a number of race conditions when the name is lost by one owner
and claimed by another. However, if no name owner currently exists,
then calls will be sent to the well-known name which may result in
the message bus launching an owner (unless
is set).
If the proxy is for a stateless D-Bus service, where the name owner may
be started and stopped between calls, the
[property
] tracking of
will cause the
proxy to drop signal and property changes from the service after it has
restarted for the first time. When interacting with a stateless D-Bus
service, do not use
— use direct D-Bus method calls and signal
connections.
The generic [signal[DBusProxy](//documentation/gio/dbusproxy/ongpropertieschanged(flags:handler:)] and
[signal[DBusProxy](//documentation/gio/dbusproxy/ongsignal(flags:handler:)] signals are not very convenient to work
with. Therefore, the recommended way of working with proxies is to subclass
, and have more natural properties and signals in your derived
class. This
shows how
this can easily be done using the
tool.
A
instance can be used from multiple threads but note
that all signals (e.g. [signal[DBusProxy](//documentation/gio/dbusproxy/ongsignal(flags:handler:)],
[signal[DBusProxy](//documentation/gio/dbusproxy/ongpropertieschanged(flags:handler:)] and
[signal
are emitted in the thread-default main
context (see [method
]) of the thread
where the instance was constructed.
An example using a proxy for a well-known name can be found in
The
type acts as a reference-counted owner of an underlying
instance.
It provides the methods that can operate on this data type through
conformance.

as a strong reference or owner of a
instance.

(instead of
) for a lightweight Swift reference to an underlying
instance.
